does: This paper evaluates vulnerabilities in large text-to-speech models by developing and testing HARMGEN, a suite of five attacks designed to generate harmful audio content. The authors identify a distinct threat beyond speaker impersonation: exploiting TTS systems to produce speech containing harmful messages. HARMGEN employs two attack families to bypass safety mechanisms: semantic obfuscation techniques (Concat, Shuffle) that conceal harmful content within text inputs, and audio-modality exploits (Read, Spell, Phoneme) that inject harmful content through auxiliary audio channels. The work demonstrates that modern LALMs can be manipulated to vocalize harmful content despite safety alignment, and identifies how real-world deployment pipelines with input/output filters can be circumvented through these techniques.
connects: This work extends prior research on TTS system misuse, particularly speaker impersonation attacks, by pivoting to content-centric threats. It builds on the broader jailbreak literature in large language models while addressing the unique challenge that TTS systems are designed to faithfully vocalize any input text, making traditional LLM jailbreaks ineffective. The paper connects to safety alignment research in foundation models and responds to growing concerns about synthetic media generation at scale. It also engages with work on input/output filtering mechanisms and multimodal safety, recognizing that single-modality defenses are insufficient when attacks can exploit alternative representations like phonemes or spelling-based injection vectors.
enables: This research enables future work on robust safety alignment for audio-generation systems and multimodal security. It highlights the need for content filtering strategies that account for semantic obfuscation and cross-modal attack vectors, informing the design of more resilient TTS deployment pipelines. The findings suggest directions for evaluating safety in foundation models beyond text, establishing benchmarks for harmful audio generation risks. The paper also motivates research into detection mechanisms for synthetic harmful speech and the development of adversarial training approaches specific to audio generation. Ultimately, it points toward the necessity of comprehensive safety frameworks that consider both the generative and multimodal nature of modern AI systems.
